summary: The Sprout Social dashboard provides a comprehensive overview of social media activity but is hampered by several UX and accessibility issues. While the information architecture is generally clear, significant accessibility violations related to color contrast and interactive elements detract from the user experience. Addressing these issues would greatly improve readability, reduce user friction, and ensure the platform is usable for a wider audience.

issues.title: Post content has insufficient text contrast
issues.problem: The gray text inside the 'Recent Posts' cards has a very low contrast ratio against the light-gray background. This makes the content difficult to read for all users and may be impossible for users with visual impairments or color vision deficiencies.
issues.critical_reason: Low-contrast text forces users to strain their eyes, leading to fatigue and reducing reading speed and comprehension. This directly violates fundamental web accessibility guidelines for legibility.
issues.recommendation: Darken the body text within the cards to meet the WCAG AA minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1. For example, use a color like #595959 or darker on the #F7F7F7 background to ensure readability.
issues.reference: WCAG 2.2 — 1.4.3 Contrast Minimum (AA): normal text requires 4.5:1 contrast ratio

issues.title: Icon-only buttons lack visible text labels
issues.problem: The buttons in the right-side vertical bar use only icons to represent actions like 'Compose', 'Notifications', and 'Search'. Without visible text labels, the meaning of these icons can be ambiguous, increasing cognitive load for users.
issues.critical_reason: This design relies on user recall rather than recognition, violating a core usability heuristic. For accessibility, if these buttons lack a proper `aria-label`, they are completely opaque to screen reader users.
issues.recommendation: Add a text label to each icon or, at minimum, provide a tooltip with the action's name that appears immediately on hover or focus. For a more inclusive design, consider a setting to permanently show labels.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Signifier: visible cues must indicate where to act

issues.title: Button border is nearly invisible
issues.problem: The border of the 'Ask a Question' button is extremely faint, providing insufficient contrast with the white background. This makes its boundary difficult to perceive, causing it to blend in and not look like a clickable element.
issues.critical_reason: The boundary of a UI component is critical for identifying it as interactive. A low-contrast border fails to meet accessibility requirements and weakens the button's affordance.
issues.recommendation: Change the border color of the button to a darker gray or the brand's blue to achieve a 3:1 contrast ratio against the background. This will make the button's boundary clearly visible and communicate its interactivity.

issues.title: Low-contrast label for sorting control
issues.problem: The 'Sort by' text label for the dropdown menu in the 'Your Recent Posts' section is a very light gray. This low contrast makes the label difficult to read and diminishes the discoverability of the sorting feature.
issues.critical_reason: Labels are essential for understanding the function of a UI control. When a label has poor legibility, it increases cognitive load and can cause users to overlook the control entirely.
issues.recommendation: Increase the contrast of the 'Sort by' label by using a darker shade of gray or black text to meet the 4.5:1 contrast ratio required by WCAG. This will ensure the label is legible to all users.

issues.title: Content overlap and ambiguous signifier in post card
issues.problem: In the second post card, hashtags are rendered on top of a gray box containing the letter 'A', making the text almost completely unreadable. Furthermore, the meaning of the gray 'A' box is unclear, acting as a poor signifier for what it represents (likely a placeholder or item type).
issues.critical_reason: This combination of a z-index rendering issue and an ambiguous icon creates visual noise and confusion. It violates the Gestalt law of Figure-Ground, as the user cannot easily distinguish the text from the background element.
issues.recommendation: Fix the layering issue so text does not overlap with other elements. Replace the ambiguous 'A' with a more descriptive icon (e.g., an image icon if it's a media placeholder) and ensure it has a tooltip explaining its purpose.

issues.title: Phone number is not a clickable link
issues.problem: The support phone number is displayed as plain text, not a clickable `tel:` link. This forces users, particularly on mobile devices, to manually copy and paste the number to make a call, creating unnecessary friction.
issues.critical_reason: This creates a gulf of execution, where the user knows what they want to do (call support) but the interface doesn't provide a direct way to do it. Reducing user effort is a key usability principle.
issues.recommendation: Wrap the phone number in an anchor tag with an `href="tel:1-866-878-3231"` attribute. This will make the number clickable, allowing users on any device to initiate a call with a single tap or click.
issues.reference: The Design of Everyday Things (Norman) — Gulf of Execution: user cannot figure out how to do what they want

issues.title: Section titles may lack semantic heading structure
issues.problem: The titles 'Your Account', 'Connect Profile', etc., are styled to look like headings but may not be implemented with semantic HTML heading tags (e.g., <h2>, <h3>). If they are not true headings, screen reader users cannot use them to navigate the page structure.
issues.critical_reason: A proper heading structure is the primary way screen reader users understand the hierarchy of a page and navigate quickly to different sections. Omitting semantic headings removes this critical navigation method.
issues.recommendation: Ensure that these visual section titles are marked up with the appropriate heading level (e.g., <h2> for 'Looking for Something Else?' and <h3> for the sub-sections like 'Your Account').
